metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exit code for the pre-sign fingerprint-mismatch path (e.g. 'exit 0 — close proceeds unsigned, not an error' or 'exit 1 — block close') in the Never-sign guard section and add it to the AC.
findings.finding: The pre-sign fingerprint-mismatch path ('close unsigned') has no stated exit code. An executor implementing the mismatch branch in `close_ticket` must choose between exit 0 (close succeeded, just unsigned) and exit 1 (block the close), with no plan guidance. This is a defaultable gap that silently affects downstream CI and agent retry logic.
findings.location: What > Never-sign guard + pre-sign fingerprint recheck; AC: 'a mismatch closes unsigned (test for the mismatch path)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'On head-SHA mismatch, `close_ticket` closes the ticket unsigned and exits 0 (the close itself succeeded; the caller is responsible for re-verifying if desired).' Then update the AC to assert the exit code, e.g. 'a mismatch closes unsigned with exit 0; the regression test asserts returncode == 0 and asserts no `sign_manifest` call was made.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document that `preflight()`-raised `LLMConfigError`/`LLMUnavailableError` exceptions do NOT carry `.outcome`; either (a) attach `.outcome` in `preflight()` via `classify_llm_failure` or (b) explicitly state that the preflight degrade path produces a plain INDETERMINATE with no disposition (correct behavior, but the plan's 'present' claim must be corrected to avoid misleading the implementer).
findings.finding: The plan's 'Reachability (verified)' note asserts that the `preflight`-path `LLMUnavailableError` has `.outcome` present (parenthetical: '`.outcome` present → disposition set'). This is false. `PydanticAIRunner.preflight()` calls `_import_pydantic_ai()` and `_pai_check_config()`, which raise `LLMConfigError`/`LLMUnavailableError` directly — these exceptions are raised BEFORE the `run()` method's `except Exception` block that executes `err.outcome = classify_llm_failure(...)`. So a preflight exception will NOT carry `.outcome`, and `getattr(error, 'outcome', None)` returns `None` → NO disposition set on the preflight path.
findings.location: Shape A — plan-review + code-review return a DEGRADED VERDICT DICT / Reachability (verified) note
findings.suggested_fix: In `PydanticAIRunner.preflight()`, after catching `LLMConfigError` or before re-raising, attach: `exc.outcome = classify_llm_failure(exc)`. Or, correct the plan text to: 'the preflight path raises without `.outcome` → plain INDETERMINATE (no disposition); only the mid-run path carries `.outcome` → disposition set.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_verify_completion()` in `_llm_commands.py` as an explicit edit site: update its `except llm.LLMError` handler to read `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` and return `11` when `outcome.retryable` is true, mirroring the same logic described for `_completion_precheck`.
findings.finding: The _verify_completion() CLI handler at _llm_commands.py:305 has its own `except llm.LLMError as exc: return 1` that catches LLMUnavailableError before any CommandError(returncode=11) from a deeper call can propagate. The plan's exit-11 text only describes the _completion_precheck → CommandError mechanism (the rebar close path), but the AC lists _verify_completion as an exit-11 site. The direct LLMError catch in _verify_completion is not named as an edit site in the plan body, leaving the _verify_completion exit-11 path underspecified.
findings.location: Exit-code-11 contract section: 'Shape B surfaces through TWO CLI entrypoints: rebar verify-completion (_verify_completion()) AND the rebar close / rebar transition … closed path'; AC: '_completion_precheck raises CommandError(returncode=11)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exit code for the fingerprint-mismatch 'close unsigned' path (e.g., exit 0 with a stderr warning 'closed unsigned: code drifted during verify→sign window') and add it to the AC and the `docs/exit-codes.md` entry.
findings.finding: The fingerprint-mismatch path ('close unsigned') has no specified exit code, but the close has already committed at that point. An agent or CI script calling `rebar close` needs to distinguish 'closed+signed' (exit 0) from 'closed+unsigned-due-to-drift' — yet the plan specifies neither. The AC only asserts 'a mismatch closes unsigned' with no returncode.
findings.location: What / Never-sign guard + pre-sign fingerprint recheck; AC: 'The pre-sign head-SHA fingerprint is re-checked inside the write lock immediately before signing; a mismatch closes unsigned (test for the mismatch path)'
findings.suggested_fix: Exit 0 with a stderr warning line is the correct choice: the ticket is legitimately closed (the write succeeded); the user must re-run `rebar review-plan` or `rebar sign` to get the attestation. Add to the AC: '...mismatch closes unsigned with exit 0 and a stderr line: Warning: closed unsigned: HEAD drifted during verify→sign window — re-sign to attest.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step describing the shape-A message rendering: name the function in `_llm_commands.py` that reads `coverage['resolution_class']`, looks it up in the message table, and writes the result to stderr — and confirm this step is covered by a test assertion on stderr output.
findings.finding: The shape-A stderr message rendering mechanism is not described in any implementation step. The AC requires that a degraded plan-review or code-review verdict prints the class-specific message (from the table) to stderr, but the plan only states the shape-B mechanism (via `CommandError.message`) and vaguely says 'shape A as a CLI-rendered line off `coverage`' without identifying which function, at which call site, does the `resolution_class → message` lookup and `stderr` write in `_llm_commands.py`.
findings.location: What > Exit-code-11 contract > Message delivery; AC: 'A degraded verdict/close prints the class-specific message (from the table) to stderr'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact stderr rendering template for shape-A gates (e.g. `rebar: {human_message} [resolution_class={rc}, retryable={r}]`) so `_review_plan` and `_review_code` produce identical formatting.
findings.finding: The plan specifies shape-A stderr message delivery as 'a CLI-rendered line off coverage' without defining the rendering format — field name, separator, or human-readable template — leaving implementers to invent inconsistent output.
findings.location: Exit-code-11 contract / Message delivery section; AC: 'A degraded verdict/close prints the class-specific message (from the table) to stderr'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a shared helper (e.g. `_format_degrade_message(coverage: dict) -> str`) in `_llm_commands.py` that reads `coverage.get('resolution_class')` and returns the matching row from the table, then call it from both `_review_plan` and `_review_code`. Specify the fallback for absent/unknown class (e.g. emit the NEEDS_INVESTIGATION message).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the runner.py module-size allowlist/ceilings entries are already present (they are, as of the repo snapshot); update the AC and scope to reflect only the `docs/architecture.md` remedy row as the remaining deliverable for this item, removing the add-to-allowlist/ceilings work that is already done.
findings.finding: runner.py is already present in both .github/module-size-allowlist.txt (line 24) and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt (line 29, ceiling=850) — the exact values the plan prescribes. The plan's deliverable 'runner.py is added to .github/module-size-allowlist.txt + module-size-ceilings.txt (850) + a docs/architecture.md remedy row' describes work that is already complete for the allowlist/ceilings portion, meaning the AC item and its verification test will silently no-op or, worse, the implementor may add a duplicate entry.
findings.location: Integration ownership section / AC: 'runner.py is added to .github/module-size-allowlist.txt + module-size-ceilings.txt (850)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC line with: 'runner.py already appears in .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and module-size-ceilings.txt (850); the remaining deliverable for this item is the docs/architecture.md remedy row (verified by test_module_size_contract.py).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the `docs/architecture.md` remedy row for `runner.py` is still absent (the allowlist+ceilings entries already exist); scope the AC to only the missing docs row rather than claiming the file registrations as new work.
findings.finding: The plan claims runner.py needs to be 'added to `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` + `module-size-ceilings.txt` (850)' as a deliverable of this story. Both files already contain this entry: `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` is on line 24 of `module-size-allowlist.txt` and has ceiling 850 in `module-size-ceilings.txt`. The plan's 'Integration ownership' section and the corresponding AC are based on a false premise that this work remains to be done.
findings.location: Integration ownership section; AC: 'runner.py is added to `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` + `module-size-ceilings.txt` (850)'
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the AC to: 'A `docs/architecture.md` remedy row exists for `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` (the allowlist and ceiling-850 entries are already present; verified by `test_module_size_contract.py`).'

findings.finding: The plan asserts `_review_code()` in `_llm_commands.py` is a shape-A gate that 'returns `code_review_verdict`' and can read `verdict['coverage'].get('resolution_class')` directly. In reality, `_review_code()` calls `llm.review_code()` → `shim.review_code()`, which translates the raw `code_review_verdict` into a `review_result` dict. The raw verdict is nested at `result['verdict']`, so the top-level `result['coverage']` does NOT contain `resolution_class` — the correct path is `result['verdict']['coverage'].get('resolution_class')`. The plan's stated data-access path for the exit-11 mapping at this CLI is wrong.
findings.location: Plan section 'CLI sites' and AC: 'rebar review-code (`_review_code()`, the code-review gate) applies the same shape-A exit-11 mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: In the CLI sites section, note that `_review_code()` obtains `coverage` from `result.get('verdict', {}).get('coverage') or {}` (the nested gate verdict), not `result.get('coverage') or {}`. Alternatively, specify that the exit-11 mapping should be added to the shim layer (`shim.review_code`) which already holds the raw verdict before translation.

findings.finding: `_review_code()` in `_llm_commands.py` returns a translated `review_result` dict (from `shim._verdict_to_review_result`), NOT the raw `code_review_verdict`. The plan directs the shape-A exit-11 mapping to read `verdict['coverage'].get('resolution_class')` at this CLI site, but `coverage` does not exist at the top level of the returned `review_result` — the raw gate verdict is nested as `result['verdict']`. Reading `result['coverage']` will silently return `None`, causing exit-11 to never trigger even on a retryable outage.
findings.location: What / Two data-flow shapes / Shape A; CLI arm `_review_code()` in `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` (lines 136-203); `src/rebar/llm/code_review/shim.py:_verdict_to_review_result`
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'CLI sites' section, update the shape-A path description for `_review_code()`: the resolution_class must be read as `result.get('verdict', {}).get('coverage', {}).get('resolution_class')` since `shim._verdict_to_review_result` wraps the raw gate verdict under a `'verdict'` key in the returned `review_result`. Alternatively, consider having `_review_code()` call `produce_code_review_verdict` directly (like the Gerrit adapter does) so it has the raw `code_review_verdict` — but note this changes the return shape of `rebar review-code`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the runner.py module-size reconciliation deliverable: the file is already in both allowlist and ceilings.txt at 850; confirm whether this wave's additions to runner.py (if any) stay under 850, and update the ceiling if not — the plan should not treat an already-present allowlist entry as a new addition.
findings.finding: runner.py is already present in both .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt (ceiling 850). The plan says to 'add' runner.py to both files and a docs/architecture.md remedy row, treating these as new edits — but the allowlist membership and ceiling already exist.
findings.location: Scope / Integration ownership section: 'runner.py was grown by five children to 824 LOC; this story owns its module-size reconciliation — added to .github/module-size-allowlist.txt + module-size-ceilings.txt (850)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'added to .github/module-size-allowlist.txt + module-size-ceilings.txt (850)' with 'already in both files at ceiling 850; confirm the wave's edits stay within 850 or raise the ceiling and add/update the docs/architecture.md remedy row if needed'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exact lookup path for `retryable` in `_review_code()` after the shim update: specify whether the shim preserves the field at `result['verdict']['coverage']` (by virtue of preserving the raw verdict dict) or additionally hoists it to `result['coverage']`, and name the exact key path `_review_code()` must read to determine exit 11.
findings.finding: The Shape-A `_review_code()` exit-11 path must read coverage from `result["verdict"]["coverage"]`, not `result["coverage"]`, but the plan's wording 'the CLI reads the persisted `retryable` bool directly' is ambiguous about this nesting. `_review_code()` receives a `review_result` (from `shim._verdict_to_review_result`), where the raw gate verdict is nested at `result["verdict"]`; coverage fields preserved onto the `review_result` by the shim live one level deeper than a flat read would expect.
findings.location: What / Shape A — plan-review + code-review; `shim._verdict_to_review_result`; `_llm_commands.py::_review_code`
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'The shim preserves coverage onto the nested verdict dict already attached as `result["verdict"]`; `_review_code()` reads `result.get("verdict", {}).get("coverage", {}).get("retryable")` to determine exit 11.' No new hoisting to top-level `result["coverage"]` is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly in the Shape A / `_review_code` section that the preserved `coverage` fields are accessed via `result['verdict']['coverage']` (the gate verdict nested by `shim._verdict_to_review_result`), OR direct AC#3 to also hoist `coverage.retryable`/`resolution_class`/`diagnostic` to the top-level `review_result` so `_review_code` can read them at `result['coverage']`.
findings.finding: The plan says `_review_code()` reads `coverage.retryable` (and `resolution_class`) 'off the preserved coverage' to map exit 11, but never specifies the access path through the translated `review_result`. `shim._verdict_to_review_result` (confirmed: line 98 of shim.py) embeds the raw gate verdict at `result['verdict'] = verdict`, so the coverage fields live at `result['verdict']['coverage']`, NOT at `result['coverage']`. An executor reading the plan's Shape A description would naturally try `result.get('coverage', {}).get('retryable')` and find nothing.
findings.location: What > Two data-flow shapes > Shape A; AC line '_review_code() distinguishes 0/1/2/11 off the preserved coverage'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the skip-signing (rather than abort) choice on HEAD drift: e.g., 'the close is legitimate (the LLM verified the correct content); only the attestation is stale — aborting the close punishes the user for network latency, while closing unsigned preserves correctness and lets the user re-sign after stabilising.'
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for the pre-sign fingerprint recheck's chosen approach (skip-signing on HEAD drift vs. abort-the-close or tighten the verify→sign window). The 'why' is stated as a threat ('code drifted during the retry/timeout-widened verify→sign window'), but the plan does not explain why skip-signing-and-close is the right response rather than aborting the whole close (with an error that lets the user retry after the drift stabilises) or reporting the drift as a warning without blocking.
findings.location: What / Never-sign guard + pre-sign fingerprint recheck

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `_completion_precheck` returns a `(manifest, verify_time_head_sha)` tuple (or equivalent) so `close_ticket` can compare `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` at sign-time to the SHA captured during verify; and clarify that the fingerprint check executes between the `transition_core` return and the `sign_manifest` call (two separate lock acquisitions), not 'inside the write lock' (which does not span both operations).
findings.finding: The pre-sign fingerprint recheck mechanism is unimplementable as specified: `_completion_precheck` returns only the manifest (a `list[str]`), not the verify-time HEAD SHA. To implement the described check ('re-read `head_sha` and compare to the verify-time value'), the verify-time SHA must be captured inside `_completion_precheck` and returned to `close_ticket` alongside the manifest — but the plan does not specify this return-type change. Additionally, the plan says 'inside the write lock, immediately before signing', but `transition_core` and `sign_manifest` use two separate write lock acquisitions; there is no single lock spanning both. The check will necessarily execute in the unlocked window between the two lock acquisitions, which is correct behaviour but not what the plan describes.
findings.location: What / Never-sign guard + pre-sign fingerprint recheck; transition_close.py `_completion_precheck` return value
findings.suggested_fix: Change the plan's return-type description: `_completion_precheck` returns `(manifest, verify_time_sha)` or `None`; `close_ticket` captures `verify_time_sha`, and immediately before calling `sign_manifest`, re-reads `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` and skips signing on mismatch. Remove the 'inside the write lock' wording and replace with 'between `transition_core` returning and `sign_manifest` being called'.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC covering the taxonomy/retry/timeout documentation deliverable: specify what document(s) are produced (e.g., a section in docs/architecture.md or a standalone doc), their required content (LLMOutcome enum values, per-class retry behavior, timeout configuration), and a verifiable test or lint check that confirms their existence.
findings.finding: The Integration ownership section names 'taxonomy/retry/timeout docs' as a deliverable this story owns ('Also owns the taxonomy/retry/timeout docs'), but no acceptance criterion covers creation, content, or verification of those documents.
findings.location: What > Integration ownership (shared files) — 'Also owns the taxonomy/retry/timeout docs.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'docs/llm-failure-taxonomy.md (or an equivalent section in docs/architecture.md) documents all 8 ResolutionClass values, their retry/timeout behavior, and the sanitization rules; verified by a link-check or existence assertion in the test suite.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR section (or extend the exit-11 ADR) documenting the MCP structured-failure return contract — the new `{error, resolution_class, retryable, diagnostic}` dict shape, why it replaces FastMCP error propagation, and the expectation that all write-gated MCP tools follow this pattern.
findings.finding: The MCP structured failure return is a new agent-facing behavioral contract (tools now return a structured dict on error instead of raising a FastMCP tool error), but no ADR or MCP tool-description/schema update is planned to document this contract for agent consumers.
findings.location: Section 'MCP structured failure return (_mcp_llm.py)' and AC bullet: '_mcp_llm.py's review_plan/review_code/verify_completion return a structured failure dict…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection to the exit-11 ADR (or a dedicated MCP-error-surface ADR) titled 'MCP Tool Error Contract' that defines the structured failure dict shape, references the three affected tools, and states the canonical pattern for future MCP tool authors. Update the MCP tool docstrings in `_mcp_llm.py` to describe the structured return on error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` (specifically `_run_plan_review`) to the Scope section as the edit site for the plan-review never-sign guard, since that is where `sign_plan_review` is called and where the `resolution_class` guard must be inserted.
findings.finding: The plan names `_review_plan()` as an exit-11 site (reading `coverage.retryable` from the verdict dict), but the sign path for plan-review lives inside `rebar.llm.plan_review.__init__._run_plan_review()`, not in `_review_plan()`. The never-sign guard 'at the plan-review sign path (`review_plan`)' should target `_run_plan_review` (or the `review_plan` public function in `plan_review/__init__.py`), not the CLI function `_review_plan`. The plan's Scope section lists `signing.py (only if a caller-side helper is factored — the guard lives at the callers)` without naming `plan_review/__init__.py` explicitly as an edit site for the never-sign guard.
findings.location: Never-sign guard section: 'at the plan-review sign path (review_plan) and the completion sign path (close_ticket), refuse to CALL sign_manifest when verdict.get("verdict") != "PASS" OR verdict.get("coverage",{}).get("resolution_class") is set'; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the plan where the sanitization allowlist/redaction rules are sourced (e.g. 'document the rules as implemented in `mamba.classify_llm_failure`; cite that function') and clarify whether 'Test coverage' means documenting existing tests or adding new ones.
findings.finding: The diagnostic-sanitization ADR's 'Allowlist + redaction rules' section is specified as a deliverable but the plan provides no source of truth for what the rules ARE (which keys are allowed, what redaction patterns apply). The plan references mamba's `classify_llm_failure` as the upstream sanitizer but does not cite where an executor should find the existing rules to document, nor whether 'Test coverage' in that ADR section implies new tests must be written.
findings.location: AC: 'The diagnostic-sanitization ADR (sections: Context, Allowlist + redaction rules, Threat model — keys/PII/hex/base64/email must never leak, Test coverage) is authored'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'The ADR's Allowlist + redaction rules section documents the rules as implemented in `mamba.classify_llm_failure` (cite the module path). Test coverage = enumerate existing mamba-side tests that exercise each rule; no new tests are added by this story unless a gap is found.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the Verdict-schema field section, enumerate the exact 8 JSON Schema `enum` values for `coverage.resolution_class` (copied from `ResolutionClass` in `failure.py`) so the executor writes a closed `"enum": ["WAIT_AND_RETRY", "RETRY_NOW", ...]` array rather than an open `"type": "string"`.
findings.finding: The plan's shape-A schema section declares `coverage.resolution_class` as 'enum, 8 values' but never enumerates the values inline for the JSON schema edit, leaving the executor to independently discover them from failure.py. A JSON Schema `enum` array requires exact string literals; without them an executor might write `type: string` with a description instead of a closed `enum: [...]`, yielding a schema that validates incorrectly.
findings.location: What > Verdict-schema field section; plan_review_verdict.schema.json / code_review_verdict.schema.json edits
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the schema section: `"resolution_class": {"type": "string", "enum": ["WAIT_AND_RETRY", "RETRY_NOW", "INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS", "CHANGE_SETTINGS", "CHANGE_INPUT", "CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL", "FIX_AGENT_DESIGN", "NEEDS_INVESTIGATION"]}`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the Session-log diagnostic write section (or the Shape B fix description), explicitly specify that for `_completion_precheck` the `append_session_log` call (wrapped in `try/except Exception`) is placed BEFORE the `raise CommandError(returncode=rc)` within the `except Exception as exc` handler.
findings.finding: The plan mandates `append_session_log(...)` at 'the shape-B `_completion_precheck` arm' but does not specify WHERE in the rewritten `_completion_precheck` body this call is placed relative to the `raise CommandError(returncode=11)`. Since `_completion_precheck` raises rather than returns, the session-log write must occur BEFORE the raise, but the plan's calling convention only says 'at each degrade site' without addressing the control-flow ordering for the raise path.
findings.location: What > Session-log diagnostic write section; What > Shape B description of _completion_precheck fix
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the shape-B calling convention: 'in `_completion_precheck`, inside the `except Exception as exc` block, call `try: rebar.append_session_log(...) except Exception: pass` BEFORE the `raise CommandError(returncode=rc) from None`'.

findings.finding: The plan specifies per-class human-facing message content (the ResolutionClass→message table) but never specifies HOW it is delivered to the user: whether it is printed to stderr, embedded in the verdict JSON, written to stdout, or some combination. For a CLI tool this is a concrete UX gap — an implementer could silently swallow the message or embed it only in a machine-readable field, leaving human operators without actionable output at exit 11.
findings.location: What → Per-class actionable messages / AC: 'A degraded verdict/close carries the class-specific message from the ResolutionClass→message table.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'The class-specific message is printed to stderr (or the CLI's standard error channel) before the process exits, in addition to being available in `coverage.diagnostic`.' This disambiguates the delivery contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the pre-sign head-SHA fingerprint recheck to the 'Why' section and scope preamble with its own rationale (e.g. 'prevent attesting a stale commit when verify→sign window allows code drift') and confirm its rollback story (recheck removed → unsigned close, no data migration).
findings.finding: The pre-sign head-SHA fingerprint recheck is a materially distinct safety concern (integrity against stale-state signing) that is not reflected in the plan title, the 'Why' goals, or the scope list's preamble — it appears only in the 'Never-sign guard' section and two ACs, giving it no stated rationale for inclusion here.
findings.location: Never-sign guard + pre-sign fingerprint recheck section; Scope bullet listing; AC: 'pre-sign head-SHA fingerprint is re-checked inside the write lock'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit sanitization posture for the `error` field in `_mcp_llm.py`'s structured failure dict — either pass it through the same sanitizer as `diagnostic`, or replace `str(exc)` with a safe summary (e.g., exception type + resolution class only), and extend the sanitization ADR to cover this field.
findings.finding: In `_mcp_llm.py`'s structured failure return, the `error` field is defined as `str(exc)` (raw exception stringification), but the plan's sanitization requirement covers only the `diagnostic` field. Provider SDK exceptions (e.g., openai, anthropic) can embed API keys, bearer tokens, internal endpoint URLs, or partial request/response content in their `__str__` representation. This unguarded field crosses the MCP trust boundary (the structured dict is returned to the driving agent/orchestrator) without a declared sanitization posture.
findings.location: MCP structured failure return section (`_mcp_llm.py`); AC: '`_mcp_llm.py`'s `review_plan`/`review_code`/`verify_completion` return a structured failure dict `{error,resolution_class,retryable,diagnostic}`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `"error": str(exc)` with `"error": f"{type(exc).__name__}: {outcome.resolution_class.value if outcome else 'unknown'}"` (type + class, no exception message content), and add a note to the diagnostic-sanitization ADR that the `error` field in MCP returns must not contain raw exception messages.

findings.finding: The AC 'The write-gated MCP LLM surface returns a structured failure result (never a raw raise) carrying the disposition, with a test for a degraded sub-path' has no corresponding implementation step in the 'What' section. The scope mentions `_mcp_writes.py` only parenthetically under the signing context; there is no described change to the MCP surface, no stated location of the 'structured failure result' shape, and no definition of what 'write-gated MCP LLM surface' means for this story. No described step reaches this AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The write-gated MCP LLM surface returns a structured failure result (never a raw raise) carrying the disposition, with a test for a degraded sub-path'; Scope — '_mcp_writes.py' (parenthetical only)

findings.finding: The AC 'The write-gated MCP LLM surface returns a structured failure result (never a raw raise) carrying the disposition, with a test for a degraded sub-path' has no corresponding description in the plan body and is absent from the Scope file list — it is an orphan criterion with no implementable specification.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last substantive AC bullet; Scope section; body sections (Two data-flow shapes, Shape A, Shape B)

findings.finding: The acceptance criterion 'The write-gated MCP LLM surface returns a structured failure result (never a raw raise) carrying the disposition, with a test for a degraded sub-path' has no corresponding What/How specification anywhere in the plan body. No section describes which MCP endpoint is affected, what 'structured failure result' means for that surface, how the disposition is serialized, or what the degraded sub-path is — leaving implementers with an unspecified AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (last bullet before lint/typecheck/test); Scope section (does not mention MCP LLM surface); What section (no MCP subsection)

findings.finding: The MCP LLM surface failure protocol (AC: 'The write-gated MCP LLM surface returns a structured failure result (never a raw raise) carrying the disposition, with a test for a degraded sub-path') has no specification in the What section. `_mcp_llm.py`'s `review_plan`, `verify_completion`, and `review_code` tools have zero `except` blocks — they call `rebar.llm.review_plan(...)` etc. directly. The plan's What section covers Shape A (gate_dispatch) and Shape B (transition_close) exhaustively but never describes the MCP surface's catch-and-return protocol: which exception types to catch, what dict shape to return, where the except block lives, or which MCP file is modified.
findings.location: What section (no MCP surface subsection) / AC: 'The write-gated MCP LLM surface returns a structured failure result' / src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py

findings.finding: `src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py` is the file that houses the MCP `review_plan`, `verify_completion`, and `review_code` tools that AC#19 ('The write-gated MCP LLM surface returns a structured failure result...') must modify, but `_mcp_llm.py` is absent from the plan's Scope section and edit-target list. The plan's structural facts mention `_mcp_writes.py` as the MCP surface, but the LLM gate tools actually live in `_mcp_llm.py` (confirmed: `mcp_server.py` line 36 imports `register_llm_tools` from `_mcp_llm`; `_mcp_writes.py` contains write/mutation tools only). Omitting `_mcp_llm.py` from Scope means the executor has no explicit mandate to modify it.
findings.location: Scope section; AC#19 ('The write-gated MCP LLM surface returns a structured failure result'); structural facts ('_mcp_writes.py')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add `_mcp_llm.py` alongside `_llm_commands.py`. In structural facts, replace 'surfaced via __init__.py / _mcp_writes.py' with 'surfaced via __init__.py / _mcp_llm.py'. Then add a brief description of what structured failure shape the three MCP tools should return on a degraded LLM call.

findings.finding: The plan identifies `_llm_commands.py` as the CLI location for Shape-B exit-11 mapping, naming only `_verify_completion()`, but the COMPLETION CLOSE GATE fires via `rebar close` → `transition.py` → `transition_close._completion_precheck` → raises `CommandError`. The exit-11 mapping for the close-gate path must be applied in the `rebar close` CLI handler (i.e., `transition.py`'s command dispatch), NOT in `_verify_completion()`. The plan's worked example says 'a 429 during completion close raises LLMUnavailableError ... → the close command exits 11' but never specifies which file/function in the close command chain reads `getattr(err, 'outcome', None)` to map it to exit 11.
findings.location: What > Shape B / Exit-code-11 contract > CLI sites section; AC#4 ('The completion close gate ... exits 11 when it is retryable')
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'CLI sites' paragraph, add: 'the `rebar close` transition CLI handler (`src/rebar/_cli/_transition_commands.py` or equivalent close arm in `transition.py`) reads `getattr(err, 'outcome', None)` off the `CommandError` raised by `_completion_precheck` and maps a retryable outcome to exit 11.' Also add this file to the Scope bullet.

findings.finding: Shape A write-site is unreachable in the mid-workflow degrade path: `produce_plan_review_verdict` calls `_degraded_plan_review_verdict(…, error=(res.error or '…'))` at gate_dispatch.py line 204-212, where `res.error` is typed `str | None` (`RunResult.error: str | None`). The original `LLMUnavailableError` exception — which carries the `.outcome` attribute — is not preserved on `RunResult`. The plan's write-site spec says the helper 'reads off the caught error's `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` (the mamba LLMOutcome)', but in this code path no exception object exists; only a string survives. The two `except LLMUnavailableError as exc:` explicit catches (lines 136, 173) do have the original exception, but the third degrade path (fallback on mid-workflow failure) does not.
findings.location: What section, 'WRITE SITE (shape A)'; src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py lines 204-212, executor.py RunResult.error

findings.finding: The plan places the never-sign structural guard in `signing.py` (`sign_manifest`), but `signing.sign_manifest(ticket_id, manifest, ...)` is a generic primitive that accepts only `ticket_id`, `manifest`, `kind`, and `repo_root` — it has no access to the verdict dict, `verdict.verdict`, or `coverage.resolution_class`. The actual sign decision (checking `verdict == PASS` and `coverage.resolution_class` is unset) already lives in `plan_review/__init__.py` (lines 482–487), not in `sign_manifest`. Placing the guard inside `sign_manifest` would require changing its public signature, breaking all 30+ existing callers (completion-verifier, tests, MCP, CLI). The plan's stated scope of 'signing.py never-sign guard' targets the wrong level of the call stack.
findings.location: Never-sign guard (structural) section; Scope section ('signing.py never-sign guard')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace 'signing.py never-sign guard' with 'plan_review/__init__.py never-sign guard (extends the existing verdict==PASS check at line 482 to also reject when coverage.resolution_class is set)'. The fingerprint recheck can attach to `attest.sign_plan_review` which already calls `signing.sign_manifest`.

findings.finding: The never-sign guard is specified to live 'at the sign decision (the `review_plan` / completion sign path), refuse to sign when `verdict.verdict != "PASS"` or `verdict.coverage.get("resolution_class")` is set' AND in `signing.py` / `sign_manifest`. But `sign_manifest(ticket_id, manifest, ...)` in `signing.py` receives only a ticket_id and a manifest list — it never receives the verdict dict. The plan does not specify a protocol for how the verdict's `coverage.resolution_class` would be visible at the `sign_manifest` call site, creating an instruction-locality gap: the guard rule and the data it needs to inspect are at different call-stack levels.
findings.location: What > Never-sign guard (structural) / src/rebar/signing.py:464-520 / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:482-501

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one observable instrument (counter metric or structured log field queryable by monitoring) for each new failure mode: exit-11 events (by resolution_class), never-sign guard firings, and MCP structured-failure returns — so operators can alert on elevated rates.
findings.finding: The plan specifies session-log writes as the sole observability instrument for the new exit-11 failure surface. No metrics, counters, or alerts are specified for operators to monitor the rate of retryable LLM failures, never-sign guard activations, or MCP structured-failure returns. In a production environment with many gate invocations, silent accumulation of exit-11 events (e.g., provider being persistently degraded) would be invisible until someone inspects logs manually.
findings.location: Section 'Session-log diagnostic write' and 'Never-sign guard'; AC lines for session-log and never-sign guard
findings.suggested_fix: At each degrade site (shape-A helpers, shape-B _completion_precheck, MCP wrapper), emit a structured log line (e.g., `logger.warning('llm_degrade', extra={'resolution_class': …, 'retryable': …, 'gate': …})`) in addition to the session-log write. For the never-sign guard, log `logger.error('never_sign_guard_activated', extra={'verdict': …, 'resolution_class': …})`. These are queryable in most log aggregators without requiring a dedicated metrics pipeline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the `head_sha(repo_root)` call inside the write lock: on any exception, treat as a mismatch (skip signing, close unsigned) and log the failure — never let a git-read failure inside the lock propagate as an unhandled exception.
findings.finding: The `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` call that performs the pre-sign fingerprint recheck is specified to run INSIDE the write lock, but the plan provides no error handling for the case where `head_sha` itself fails (e.g., git subprocess error, filesystem unavailability). An unhandled exception escaping the write-lock body would propagate as an unexpected crash from `close_ticket`, potentially with a confusing traceback rather than a clean CommandError, and the write lock's release depends entirely on whether the implementation uses a context manager.
findings.location: Section 'Never-sign guard + pre-sign fingerprint recheck'; 'close_ticket' caller; AC: 'The pre-sign head-SHA fingerprint is re-checked inside the write lock immediately before signing'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the head_sha call: `try: current_sha = signing.head_sha(repo_root) except Exception as e: logger.error('head_sha_recheck_failed', exc_info=e); current_sha = None` — then the mismatch branch (`current_sha != verify_time_sha` or `current_sha is None`) skips signing and closes unsigned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (in the plan or via a pointer to existing `append_session_log` documentation) that `append_session_log` is atomic under concurrent callers, or note that log-entry corruption is an accepted risk for the best-effort write.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `append_session_log` is safe for concurrent invocations. If multiple gate commands run in parallel (e.g., a CI job reviewing several tickets simultaneously) and all hit LLM degradation, concurrent calls to `append_session_log` could produce interleaved or truncated log entries. The best-effort wrap (`try/except Exception`) handles the case where the write raises but not the case where concurrent writes corrupt individual entries.
findings.location: Section 'Session-log diagnostic write (calling convention, both shapes)'; AC: 'a session-log failure does not fail the gate'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether FastMCP `ToolError` can carry structured metadata (e.g., `data` field or exception chaining) as the established error channel, and either use it or document in the exit-11 ADR why the return-dict approach is preferred over the MCP-standard error path.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel MCP tool behavioral contract — returning a structured failure dict `{error, resolution_class, retryable, diagnostic}` from the LLM gate tools on `LLMError` instead of raising a FastMCP tool error — without citing prior art for this 'errors-as-data' pattern or verifying whether FastMCP's `ToolError` mechanism supports structured payloads (which would be the established approach). The MCP spec and FastMCP have a well-defined error channel; the plan neither evaluates nor rejects it.
findings.location: MCP structured failure return section (`_mcp_llm.py`); AC: 'return a structured failure dict {error,resolution_class,retryable,diagnostic} on a raised LLMError instead of propagating a FastMCP tool error'
findings.suggested_fix: Check whether `mcp.server.fastmcp.exceptions.ToolError` (or the MCP `CallToolResult` error variant) accepts a structured `data` payload. If yes, prefer `raise ToolError(str(exc), data={"resolution_class": ..., "retryable": ..., "diagnostic": ...})` so agents can branch on the MCP-standard error channel without changing their success-path handler. If the structured data field is absent or poorly supported in the current FastMCP version, document the constraint in the ADR and note that the return-dict approach is a deliberate workaround.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
